1: 'Finally, brothers, pray for us, that the word of the Lord may speed ahead and be honored, as happened among you.' The 'word of the Lord' (logos tou kyriou) is the gospel of Jesus — Christ himself as the content of proclamation. Paul's prayer request is that this Christ-word advance swiftly and be glorified, as it was in Thessalonica. The honor due the word is honor due to Christ who is the word's content.
2: 'And that we may be delivered from wicked and evil men. For not all have faith.' The deliverance Paul requests is from those who oppose the gospel — those who resist Christ. The phrase 'not all have faith' (ou gar pantōn hē pistis) recalls that faith in Paul is always faith in Christ; those without it stand outside the community constituted by him. The apostolic ministry operates in a world divided by its response to Jesus.
3: 'But the Lord is faithful. He will establish you and guard you against the evil one.' The Lord's faithfulness (pistos de estin ho kyrios) is the foundation of the assurance against the evil one — the same adversary whose activity was described in 2 Thessalonians 2. Christ is the guardian against the eschatological opponent; his faithfulness is his covenant reliability to protect those who belong to him. The Christological counterweight to the mystery of lawlessness is the faithfulness of the Lord.
4: 'And we have confidence in the Lord about you, that you are doing and will do the things that we command.' The confidence (pepoithamen en kyriō) is located 'in the Lord' — the Pauline shorthand for the sphere of Christ's Lordship and the dynamic of his Spirit. Ethical confidence about the community is grounded not in human assessment but in Christ's work among them. Their obedience is the fruit of being in him.
5: 'May the Lord direct your hearts to the love of God and to the steadfastness of Christ.' The steadfastness of Christ (hē hypomonē tou Christou) is Christ's own endurance — his patient suffering under opposition, his endurance of the cross for the joy set before him (Hebrews 12:2). Believers are directed into his steadfastness as a pattern to inhabit. This is ethical Christological formation: not merely imitation from outside but Spirit-mediated participation in Christ's own patient endurance.
6: 'Now we command you, brothers, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that you keep away from any brother who is walking in idleness.' Commands issued 'in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ' invoke Christ's full apostolic authority — he is the source and sanction of the instruction. The Lordship of Christ extends into community discipline; the church order Paul establishes is Christologically grounded.
7: 'For you yourselves know how you ought to imitate us, because we were not idle when we were with you.' The apostolic example (mimeisthai) functions as a Christological imitation chain: Paul imitates Christ (1 Corinthians 11:1: imitate me as I imitate Christ); the Thessalonians imitate Paul. The work-ethic Paul models is the self-giving, other-serving pattern of Christ's own ministry, reproduced through the apostolic example.
8: 'Nor did we eat anyone's bread without paying for it, but with toil and labor we worked night and day, that we might not be a burden to any of you.' Paul's deliberate non-burdening echoes Christ's own self-giving posture — the one who came not to be served but to serve (Mark 10:45). The apostle embodies the Christological pattern of voluntary self-limitation for the benefit of others; the night-and-day labor is a cruciform existence.
9: 'It was not because we do not have that right, but to give you in ourselves an example to imitate.' The right (exousia) foregone is real apostolic authority — yet Paul sets it aside as Christ set aside divine prerogative (Philippians 2:6–7: did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped). The voluntary renunciation of rights for the sake of others is the kenotic Christological pattern enacted in apostolic labor. The example given is the pattern of Christ embodied.
10: 'For even when we were with you, we would give you this command: If anyone is not willing to work, let him not eat.' The dominical-flavored command enforces the creation order of labor — but in the context of a community awaiting Christ's return, the command also expresses that eschatological expectation does not dissolve present creaturely responsibility. Christ's coming does not suspend the order he created; faithful waiting is active, not passive.
11: 'For we hear that some among you walk in idleness, not busy at work, but busybodies.' The idleness (ataktōs peripatountas) is a failure to walk in a manner worthy of the calling (Ephesians 4:1). The Christological calling is to walk as Christ walked — in purposeful love and service, not anxious inactivity disguised as spiritual vigilance. The busybodies invert the Christological work-pattern.
12: 'Now such persons we command and encourage in the Lord Jesus Christ to do their work quietly and to earn their own living.' The command is again issued 'in the Lord Jesus Christ' — his Lordship frames the instruction. Quiet, steady work ('earn their own living,' hēsychian ergazomenoi) reflects the Christological pattern of faithful, unremarkable service that does not draw attention to itself. The self-giving of daily labor is the ordinary expression of cruciform existence.
13: 'As for you, brothers, do not grow weary in doing good.' The exhortation against weariness echoes Galatians 6:9 (let us not grow weary in doing good, for in due season we will reap if we do not give up). The harvest metaphor implies eschatological vindication: the good done in Christ's name will be recognized at his return. Patient, unrewarded goodness is sustained by eschatological hope in Christ.
14: 'If anyone does not obey what we say in this letter, take note of that person, and have nothing to do with him, that he may be ashamed.' The shame aimed at (entrépō — to turn; hence to be turned in on oneself in reflection) is corrective, not punitive. The goal of community discipline is restoration — the shamed person turned back to Christ and community. This reflects the Christological goal of all church discipline: reclamation rather than rejection.
15: 'Do not regard him as an enemy, but warn him as a brother.' The limit of discipline is fraternal love — the disciplined person remains a brother, not an adversary. This reflects the Christological ethic of Matthew 18:15–17, where the goal of correction is to 'gain your brother.' The community constituted by Christ's love does not abandon its members; it pursues them with the corrective purpose of restoration to Christ.
16: 'Now may the Lord of peace himself give you peace at all times in every way. The Lord be with you all.' The Lord of peace (ho kyrios tēs eirēnēs) identifies Jesus as the giver of the peace he himself embodied — the shalom proclaimed by the angels at his birth (Luke 2:14), achieved by his blood (Colossians 1:20), and breathed on the disciples after his resurrection (John 20:19–21: Peace be with you). The benediction applies to Christ the eschatological peace of God.
17: 'I, Paul, write this greeting with my own hand. This is the sign of genuineness in every letter of mine. This is how I write.' The autograph marks the letter as authentically Pauline — a counter-measure against the forged letter of 2 Thessalonians 2:2. The apostolic authentication system protects the integrity of the Christ-proclamation: false letters claiming Paul's authority corrupt the gospel. Paul's hand guarantees the Christological content is genuinely transmitted.
18: 'The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all.' The final benediction is identical to the closing formula of 1 Thessalonians (2 Thessalonians 5:28) and nearly all Pauline letters. Grace (charis) is the shorthand for the entire saving work of Christ — his death, resurrection, and ongoing presence. The closing prayer is that this Christological grace permeate and sustain the whole community. The letter ends where it began: in Christ, from Christ, toward Christ.
